title: Monty Cant Buy Me Love

jokes.text: This gentleman's beer tap dates back to the turn of the century... and remarkably, seems to have never been washed. Yeah, yeah, I've been meaning to wash that, but- it's been such a century.
jokes.setup: Appraiser notes antique beer tap hasn't been washed in 100 years
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual excuse that he's been too busy for an entire century
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - treating a century like a busy week. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Appraiser
dialogue.line: This antique beer tap hasn't been washed in 100 years.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I've been busy.

jokes.text: At auction, I'd expect this to bring $20,000 to $30,000... except that on the handle somebody's carved 'Homer rocks.' And I do. Whoo! Appraised value: $15.
jokes.setup: Valuable antique worth tens of thousands
jokes.punchline: Homer's vandalism reduces it to $15, and he's proud of it
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer destroying valuable things through ignorance, with perfect confidence

dialogue.speaker: Appraiser
dialogue.line: This is a valuable antique worth tens of thousands of dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow! That much, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Appraiser
dialogue.line: Yes, if you're very careful with it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, I will be. I promise.

dialogue.speaker: Appraiser
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be back in a week to appraise it again.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See you then!

dialogue.speaker: Appraiser
dialogue.line: Well, what happened to it?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I may have sat on it. And spilled some beer on it. And used it to prop up the couch.

dialogue.speaker: Appraiser
dialogue.line: This is worth fifteen dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fifteen dollars?! But I didn't break it!

jokes.text: Oh, we stopped those when the kids said I was too fat to carry.
jokes.setup: Marge asks when they last went for a family walk
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals the cruel reason they stopped - kids couldn't carry his weight
jokes.explanation: Dark family dysfunction delivered matter-of-factly. Strong economy and character truth.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: When was the last time we went for a family walk?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we stopped after the kids couldn't carry my weight anymore.

jokes.text: I'm not lazy. I'm just, um, uh- Lisa, finish my sentence for me. Why don't you finish your own darn- [Snoring]
jokes.setup: Bart claims he's not lazy while asking Lisa to finish his sentence
jokes.punchline: Lisa falls asleep mid-protest, proving the family's laziness
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic structure - asking someone else to finish your denial of laziness

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm not lazy, Lisa. Just finish this sentence for me.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that's the definition of—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: zzzzz...

jokes.text: All this fresh air is making my hair move... and I don't know how much longer I can complain.
jokes.setup: Family complaining about being outside
jokes.punchline: Bart's meta-awareness that he's running out of complaints
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about complaining while actively complaining. High originality and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, there's nothing to complain about out here. No traffic, no smog, no annoying people...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. I'm running out of complaints. That's the worst thing that could happen to me!

jokes.text: There's some dogs. We could all ride dogs. Forget it. Nobody's riding any- Hi-yo, Silver, away!
jokes.setup: Homer suggests riding dogs, Marge shuts it down
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately jumps on a dog despite the refusal
jokes.explanation: Classic family dynamics - kid doing exactly what parent forbid

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We could ride the dogs!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Giddyup!

jokes.text: But you don't wanna buy your buttons there. Whew. Well, I dodged a bullet. Now there's the place you wanna buy your buttons.
jokes.setup: Marge giving detailed walking tour commentary about button stores
jokes.punchline: Homer's relief at avoiding wrong button store, as if it's life-threatening
jokes.explanation: Absurd specificity of button store geography treated as crucial information

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And here we have Schmidt's Button Emporium, established in 1987. Notice the authentic brass fixtures and the hand-blown glass display cases. The owner, Klaus Schmidt, personally curates each button selection. They specialize in vintage mother-of-pearl buttons from the early 20th century.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh-huh.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now, if we take a left here, we can avoid Kowalski's Button World entirely. Their inventory is simply pedestrian, and their customer service is abysmal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Whew! That was close. *wipes forehead* For a second there, I thought we were actually going to go in there.

jokes.text: If you love me, you'll kill me.
jokes.setup: Marge continuing tedious walking tour commentary
jokes.punchline: Homer's deadpan plea for death as escape from boredom
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and structure. Dark humor delivered with perfect deadpan timing.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And this is the old Simpson homestead, built in 1895 by Jebediah Springfield's third cousin twice removed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Kill me.

jokes.text: 'Mega' means good, and 'store' means thing.
jokes.setup: Someone explains it's not just a store, it's a megastore
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely wrong but confident etymology
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer pseudo-intellectualism. Perfect character integration with made-up etymology.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Megastore? Mega... like in Megaman? So it's a store run by a blue robot?

jokes.text: Whoa! This place has everything... even a Shoplifting Department. What convenience. I'm doin' all my thievin' here.
jokes.setup: Characters impressed by megastore's comprehensiveness
jokes.punchline: Store has organized department for criminal activity, Snake appreciates the convenience
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept - crime as customer service. Perfect for Snake's character.

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Wow, this store has everything! Even a section for criminal activity!

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Dude, that's like, so convenient. I don't even have to case the joint anymore.

jokes.text: Man, all these bands are just rippin' off Judas Priest.
jokes.setup: Bart looking at music in store
jokes.punchline: 10-year-old has oddly specific heavy metal knowledge and opinions
jokes.explanation: Funny contrast between Bart's age and sophisticated metal critique

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, this Metallica album is like, totally overrated. They sold out after the Black Album.

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: You're ten years old.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and? I've been listening to doom metal since I was eight. Slayer's early work was pure genius.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. I am really sorry. Ugh! Ugh! I don't know what I was thinking... but, uh, Field of Dreams was good, wasn't it? Made us all believe again. Oh, poor Mr. Costner. He tries so hard.
jokes.setup: Director's commentary on bad movie
jokes.punchline: Kevin Costner pathetically apologizing for his own movie
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about celebrity commentary tracks and Costner's career struggles

dialogue.speaker: Kevin Costner
dialogue.line: I know what you're thinking: 'Kevin, how could you make such a terrible movie?' Well, I ask myself that every morning. I wake up, look in the mirror, and say, 'Kevin, you magnificent fool, you've done it again.' This film represents everything I stand against as an artist, and yet here it is, a monument to my own incompetence. I'm sorry. I'm so, so sorry.

jokes.text: Ah, thanks. You're sweet to say that. Uh, where are you? I'm back here. Hi. Will you bring me a sandwich? Please? No-No crusts.
jokes.setup: Costner responds to Marge's sympathy through the TV
jokes.punchline: He's somehow actually there and asking for sandwich service
jokes.explanation: Surreal escalation from commentary to actual presence, with pathetic sandwich request

dialogue.speaker: Kevin Costner
dialogue.line: Thank you for your sympathy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You're welcome.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin Costner
dialogue.line: Say, do you have any sandwiches?

jokes.text: Step lively, Smithers. That orphanage won't demolish itself.
jokes.setup: Burns arriving somewhere in a hurry
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of demolishing an orphanage as routine business
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns line - casual evil delivered with business efficiency. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm in a terrible hurry. I have to demolish an orphanage.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, you demolished an orphanage last week.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, this one's in the way of my plutonium storage facility. Besides, orphans have no one to complain to the authorities.

jokes.text: Once again, my underwear has become tangled in a cowcatcher. Ooh!
jokes.setup: Burns deploying cowcatcher to move crowd
jokes.punchline: Ned's matter-of-fact reaction to recurring underwear entanglement
jokes.explanation: Absurd specificity of recurring problem, delivered in Ned's mild manner

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'd shake your hand, but my underwear's caught in the car door again.

jokes.text: Books and cocoa in the same store? What's next, a talking banana? Uh, I don't see one, sir. No, of course not. The very notion of a talking banana is absurd. Still-
jokes.setup: Burns amazed by combined bookstore/cafe concept
jokes.punchline: His escalation to talking banana, then genuine consideration of the idea
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns logic - dismissing idea while secretly considering it. Strong absurdist escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: A bookstore AND a cafe? Ingenious! Why, it's like a talking banana.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, a talking banana doesn't make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You're right, Smithers. A bookstore and a cafe... that's actually brilliant.

jokes.text: I went down Mount Everest on a boogie board. Climbed Niagara Falls. And just last month, I knocked out Muhammad Ali.
jokes.setup: Fortune listing his exciting accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Escalation from extreme sports to beating up elderly boxing legend
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure, dark turn with Ali reference

dialogue.speaker: Arthur Fortune
dialogue.line: I've bungee jumped off the Golden Gate Bridge, I've piloted a fighter jet, I've wrestled a grizzly bear...

dialogue.speaker: Arthur Fortune
dialogue.line: And just last week, I beat up Muhammad Ali!

jokes.text: It seems I have too much money. Who wants a dollar?
jokes.setup: Fortune announcing bad news from accountant
jokes.punchline: The 'problem' of having too much money
jokes.explanation: Good setup for rich person obliviousness to normal financial problems

dialogue.speaker: Arthur Fortune
dialogue.line: I've just received word from my accountant that I have a serious problem.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur Fortune
dialogue.line: I have too much money.

jokes.text: I don't know. Just give me the dollar.
jokes.setup: Fortune asks child's name before giving dollar
jokes.punchline: Ralph's direct, transactional response ignoring social niceties
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph logic - cutting through social interaction to get to the point

dialogue.speaker: Fortune Teller
dialogue.line: What is your name, child?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Ralph.

jokes.text: This goes right in the old poor box. Not so fast, old chum.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy about to donate money to charity
jokes.punchline: Burns intercepting money intended for the poor
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns move - stealing from charity. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: I'm about to donate this money to charity for the poor.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Not so fast, Lovejoy! That money is coming with me!

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: But Mr. Burns, this is for those in need!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: In need? The only thing they need is to work harder. Now if you'll excuse me, Smithers and I have a money vault to fill.

jokes.text: Where's the dignity? Where's the contempt for the common man?
jokes.setup: Burns criticizing Arthur Fortune's behavior
jokes.punchline: Burns thinks contempt for people is dignified behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns worldview - contempt as a virtue. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Contempt. Yes, that's the mark of a dignified man. Someone who looks down on the common rabble with undisguised disdain.

jokes.text: Bah! [Echoing] Bah! Bah! Bah!
jokes.setup: News describing classic miser brooding in mansion
jokes.punchline: Cut to Burns literally doing exactly that with echoing 'bah'
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual gag - news describes exact scene we then see. Great character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Bah! Bah! Bah!

jokes.text: I thought I had everything- money, good looks, strong, sharp teeth.
jokes.setup: Burns listing his supposed assets
jokes.punchline: Including 'strong, sharp teeth' as major life achievement
jokes.explanation: Absurd inclusion of dental status among life's greatest achievements

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My teeth are strong and sharp, a major life achievement.

jokes.text: Free silver dollars, compliments of C. Montgomery Burns! [People screaming and getting hurt by coins]
jokes.setup: Burns trying to copy Fortune's dollar-giving success
jokes.punchline: Silver dollars being heavy enough to injure people when thrown
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy based on Burns' lack of understanding of practical consequences

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, fetch me some silver dollars. If Oprah can give away cars, I shall give away... silver dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Very good, sir. But sir, silver dollars are quite heavy.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! Distribute them at once!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Hey! Watch it!

jokes.text: Ow! Ow! Take it out! Take it out! No! Ooh! Put it back! Put it back!
jokes.setup: Homer apparently has silver dollar embedded in his head
jokes.punchline: His contradictory demands about whether to remove it
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer injury comedy with typical indecisiveness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't remove it! It's a silver dollar!

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: But Mr. Simpson, it's lodged in your brain.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then remove it! What are you, a doctor or a nickel-and-dimer?

jokes.text: Maybe you should see a doctor about that coin in your brain. Maybe you should mind your own business. Afternoon, miss.
jokes.setup: Moe suggests medical attention for obvious head injury
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual dismissal and polite greeting as if nothing's wrong
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to normalize any injury or situation. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Homer, you got a hole in your head. You should see a doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nah, I'm fine. How ya doin', Moe?

jokes.text: I'm behind Adam Sandler, for God's sake.
jokes.setup: Burns checking billionaire popularity rankings
jokes.punchline: Adam Sandler being more popular than him is the ultimate insult
jokes.explanation: Cultural reference that works for Burns' character but somewhat dated

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Adam Sandler? More popular than me? Why, that's... that's the ultimate insult!

jokes.text: Wait a minute. Because I brought the check, they named a wing after me? [Laughing]
jokes.setup: Homer realizes he got credit for Burns' donation
jokes.punchline: His delight at accidentally stealing credit
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious enjoyment of unearned recognition fits his character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns gave a million dollars to the orphanage? And everyone thinks I did it?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I'm a hero! And I didn't have to give up a single donut!

jokes.text: Well, I will be when the morphine wears off, but until then-
jokes.setup: Burns saying Homer must be mad about the situation
jokes.punchline: Homer admitting he's only calm due to medical drugs
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with Homer's casual drug use affecting his emotional response

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You must be furious about this situation, Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I would be, sir, but I'm on so many prescription drugs right now, I can't feel a thing.

jokes.text: Oh, get with the times, man. It's Jerry Rude and the Bathroom Bunch.
jokes.setup: Burns thinks Don McNeill's Breakfast Club is still popular
jokes.punchline: Modern radio is crude shock jock named Jerry Rude
jokes.explanation: Satirical name for shock jock radio, but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Don McNeill's Breakfast Club is still popular, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, sir, modern radio is just a crude shock jock named Jerry Rude.

jokes.text: I wrote some jokes about how white people are different from black people.
jokes.setup: Jerry offering Burns backup material for the show
jokes.punchline: His 'jokes' are lazy racial observation comedy
jokes.explanation: Satire of hack stand-up comedy, but the joke itself is weak

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Rude
dialogue.line: I got some backup material for ya. You know what I noticed about Chinese people? They're always doing math!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Rude
dialogue.line: And black guys? They love fried chicken! What's up with that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Rude
dialogue.line: Jews are cheap! Have you noticed that? It's true!

jokes.text: You see, white people have names like Lenny... whereas black people have names like Carl.
jokes.setup: Jerry's terrible racial humor setup
jokes.punchline: The 'different' names are just two regular names from the show
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of racial comedy by using completely ordinary names from the show's own characters

jokes.text: All right. Uh, how many times a day do you go to the can? Oh, about 40, I suppose. When are we going on the air? We're on the air now, Skeletor.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking crude questions Burns doesn't understand
jokes.punchline: Burns answering honestly about bathroom frequency while not realizing they're broadcasting
jokes.explanation: Burns' old-fashioned confusion creating unintentional TMI moment

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Rude
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, how many times a day do you use the bathroom?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, that's a rather personal question, but I suppose... three times? Four on Tuesdays. Why do you ask?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Rude
dialogue.line: No reason, just curious about your bowel movements.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Bowel movements? Good heavens, what a topic for discussion! Though I must say, regularity is the hallmark of a well-ordered life.

jokes.text: Oh, well, when I was six, my father took me on a picnic. That was a gay old time. Ho-ho! I ate my share of wieners that day.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks about Burns' 'first gay experience'
jokes.punchline: Burns innocently describes childhood picnic using old-fashioned language that sounds sexual
jokes.explanation: Excellent double entendre based on Burns' archaic vocabulary. Perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So Mr. Burns, tell us about your first gay experience.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Ah yes, well it was a beautiful spring day. I was but a lad of seven, and my father took me on a picnic. We spread out a blanket beneath the elm trees and unpacked a lovely spread of cold meats and cheeses. Then he said, 'Come here, boy, let's play with my stick.' We played with that stick for hours! It was the most gay and carefree afternoon of my youth.

jokes.text: Most of the sounds you are now hearing are not being made by me.
jokes.setup: Continuous farting sounds during interview
jokes.punchline: Burns' formal disclaimer about the sound effects
jokes.explanation: Burns' formal language used for undignified situation. Good character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I want to make it clear that those sounds are a result of my advanced age and diet, not some sort of comedic device. This interview is serious business.

jokes.text: Wow! A blue car!
jokes.setup: Burns says Homer isn't easily impressed
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately proves him wrong by being amazed by ordinary car
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate contradiction of Burns' statement. Excellent economy and character truth.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You're not easily impressed, are you Homer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow! A car!

jokes.text: A sober Irishman? Even rarer.
jokes.setup: Burns suggests bringing people something rare
jokes.punchline: Ethnic stereotype delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Relies on ethnic stereotype, though fits Burns' character. Low craft score for lazy writing.

jokes.text: Peter Graves couldn't find ugly at a Radcliffe mixer.
jokes.setup: Homer mentions Nimoy and Graves failed to find monster
jokes.punchline: Burns' specific insult about Peter Graves and elite college women
jokes.explanation: Excellent Burns insult - specific, elitist, and unexpectedly clever structure

jokes.text: They still have the same pool table on which I was conceived, born and educated.
jokes.setup: Willie talking about his parents' tavern
jokes.punchline: His entire life cycle happened on one piece of furniture
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation revealing Willie's entire backstory. Excellent economy and dark humor.

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Me parents owned a tavern. I was conceived on the pool table, born on the bar, and me father died in the corner booth.

jokes.text: They still have the same pool table on which I was conceived, born and educated.
jokes.setup: Willie explaining his parents own a tavern
jokes.punchline: Entire life cycle happening on one piece of furniture
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor showing Willie's impoverished background with perfect escalation

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Me parents own a tavern. I was born on a pool table, conceived on a pool table, and I'll probably die on a pool table. The entire cycle of life, happening on one piece of furniture.

jokes.text: So you're back, Son. Aye. I suppose you'll be leavin' soon. Aye. Good.
jokes.setup: Willie reuniting with his father
jokes.punchline: Extremely brief, unwelcoming family reunion
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy showing dysfunctional family dynamic in minimal dialogue

dialogue.speaker: Willie's Father
dialogue.line: Willie.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Dad!

dialogue.speaker: Willie's Father
dialogue.line: Aye.

jokes.text: You're not payin' me anything! You kidnapped me. I remember it distinctly... with the grabbing and duct taping and the tennis ball in the mouth. It hurt me.
jokes.setup: Burns asking what he's paying Homer for
jokes.punchline: Homer matter-of-factly describing his kidnapping with childlike complaint
jokes.explanation: Homer's casual description of trauma with focus on minor physical discomfort

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: What exactly am I paying you for?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you kidnapped me, so I figured you owed me money. Plus, you never even gave me a snack on the drive over here. I'm starving!

jokes.text: Oh, good glavin! It's on my shoe! It's a- a small frog. Just get off. Just get off there. Just get out. Get out!
jokes.setup: Frink detecting giant monster on his equipment
jokes.punchline: It's just a tiny frog that he can't handle
jokes.explanation: Classic scientist character unable to handle simple real-world situation

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Good heavens! The readings indicate a creature of absolutely gargantuan proportions!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: It appears to be heading directly toward us!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Wait... the scale is recalibrating...

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Oh my, yes. It's just a tiny frog. Gosh, I can't handle this.

jokes.text: Can you believe I'm a size four? Whoo!
jokes.setup: Homer wearing a kilt in Scotland
jokes.punchline: His pride in fitting into tiny dress size
jokes.explanation: Homer's misunderstanding of sizing systems combined with body image delusion

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at me! I'm wearing a size 2! I'm finally a woman!

jokes.text: Can you believe I'm a size four? Whoo! Ach. Aye.
jokes.setup: Homer is wearing a kilt
jokes.punchline: He's excited about fitting in small size while Scots are disgusted
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy with Homer's pride contrasted against Scottish disapproval.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Look at me, I'm a real Scot now!

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Och, ye dinnae have the faintest idea what ye're doin', do ye?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm wearing the kilt, I'm fitting right in!

dialogue.speaker: Willie's Father
dialogue.line: Ye cannae just wear the tartan and expect to be one of us, ye eejit!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I got it in a small size and it fits perfectly!

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: That's the problem, ye numpty. A true Scotsman would never squeeze intae such a wee thing.

jokes.text: We've lost our homes and everything we hold dear. Aye.
jokes.setup: Lake being drained destroys local community
jokes.punchline: Understated Scottish response to total devastation
jokes.explanation: Stereotypical Scottish stoicism in face of disaster

dialogue.speaker: Scottish Resident
dialogue.line: Well, that's not very good.

jokes.text: No way! Aberdeen rules!
jokes.setup: Discovering what appears to be school float instead of monster
jokes.punchline: Student defending school pride despite circumstances
jokes.explanation: Absurd school spirit in wrong context, but not particularly strong

dialogue.speaker: Aberdeen Student
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, at least it's not as bad as that thing that attacked us last week.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, that was the school float.

dialogue.speaker: Aberdeen Student
dialogue.line: Exactly! And it still looks better than our school!

jokes.text: I was a little worried when he swallowed me... but, well, you know the rest.
jokes.setup: Homer describing his encounter with the monster
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to being swallowed alive
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to downplay extraordinary experiences

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So there I was, face to face with this huge monster. And you know what happened? It swallowed me whole! But no big deal, I've had worse Tuesdays.

jokes.text: the eighth being Gomer Pyle's heavenly singing voice
jokes.setup: Listing wonders of the world
jokes.punchline: Gomer Pyle's singing voice as eighth wonder
jokes.explanation: Random cultural reference without much payoff

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Gomer Pyle's singing voice as the eighth wonder of the world.

jokes.text: Yes, that's it. Let it all out. Clutch me to your common bosom.
jokes.setup: Burns finally getting the adulation he wanted
jokes.punchline: His creepy, condescending way of expressing enjoyment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns line - enjoying love while still being condescending. Excellent character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent. I'm enjoying this moment of universal acclaim. Tell me, how does it feel to bask in the presence of greatness?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, great, Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You may now touch my sleeve. Briefly.

jokes.text: Well, if you wanted people to love you... you sure blew it with that insane rampage.
jokes.setup: Monster went on destructive rampage
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact critique of Burns' PR disaster
jokes.explanation: Homer's direct assessment of situation with typical bluntness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Mr. Burns, I've seen a lot of things in my day, but that monster destroying half the town? That's gonna be tough to spin. You're gonna need more than a press conference and some donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, what would you suggest?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I dunno, blame Flanders?

jokes.text: I'm a selfish old crank, and that fits me like a Speedo.
jokes.setup: Burns realizing he should accept who he is
jokes.punchline: Comparing personality to uncomfortable swimwear
jokes.explanation: Excellent metaphor combining self-awareness with specific visual image. Great character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You know, I've spent my entire life trying to change who I am. But perhaps I should accept myself as I am.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: After all, fighting your true nature is like wearing a Speedo two sizes too small — eventually, everyone around you notices, and you just look ridiculous.

jokes.text: So what do we do with our friend here? Uh, throw him in the Dumpster? No, no, no. I really want to give the lovable scamp a good home.
jokes.setup: Deciding what to do with the monster
jokes.punchline: Burns' sudden affection for creature that just terrorized everyone
jokes.explanation: Burns' typical self-serving sentimentality after disaster

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, look at the poor creature. It's been through so much.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, it just destroyed half the town.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I've decided to adopt it. We shall be the best of friends.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Mr. Burns, that thing almost killed us all!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Homer, you simply don't understand the finer points of monster appreciation.

jokes.text: Okay, okay. Want a shrimp cocktail? Nah. Yeah, they're not great.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to appease the monster
jokes.punchline: Casual conversation with monster about cocktail party food quality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - treating monster like dinner guest. Good absurdist logic.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Nessie, how about some of these little weenie things? They're pretty good.

dialogue.speaker: Nessie
dialogue.line: Meh.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know what you mean. They're not that great.

tmdb_overview: A new mogul wins the hearts of the townspeople with a shower of cash. Jealous, Mr. Burns asks Homer to help make him a beloved billionaire too, and they hatch a plan to bring the Loch Ness Monster to Springfield.
insight: Monty's buying spree delivers 54 jokes at 2.44-per-minute pace, anchored by character comedy.
